--- title: "MC1: TenantThread Embargo Breach - Visual Analytics" subtitle: "VAST Challenge 2026 - Multi-Agent Crisis Investigation" author: "Wu Jiayan" date: "May 26, 2026" date-modified: "last-modified" format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-title: "Contents" toc-location: right code-fold: true code-summary: "Show R Code" self-contained: true fig-width: 16 fig-height: 8 fig-dpi: 150 embed-resources: true execute: eval: true echo: true warning: false freeze: true out-width: "100%" --- ::: {.cell} ::: # 1 Introduction On June 5, 2046, information about the CivicLoom-TenantThread merger (Project HarborCrest) surfaced publicly before the contractual 6 PM embargo lift. This report presents visual analytics of **912 agent messages across 23 rounds** to answer the Mini-Challenge question: did TenantThread's team deliberately leak the merger, or did the communications system break down under pressurex # 2 Preparation ## 2.1 Loading Packages This chunk loads the R packages used throughout the report. The package set covers JSON parsing, data wrangling, date handling, table formatting, text mining, network construction, and interactive visualisation. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pacman::p_load( jsonlite, dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, scales, forcats, patchwork, lubridate, stringr, knitr, kableExtra, igraph, ggraph, tidytext, ggrepel, plotly, gganimate, glue, htmltools, htmlwidgets ) ``` ::: **Data Manipulation & Cleanup** - **`dplyr`**: Manipulates data frames using intuitive verbs like filtering, mutating, and summarizing. - **`tidyr`**: Reshapes data into a "tidy" format where every column is a variable and every row is an observation. - **`stringr`**: Simplifies working with text strings and regular expressions using consistent functions. - **`lubridate`**: Makes parsing, manipulating, and doing arithmetic with dates and times much easier. - **`forcats`**: Solves common problems with factors, which handle categorical variables in R. - **`glue`**: Combines strings and R expressions safely and easily without using complex punctuation. **Static Data Visualization** - **`ggplot2`**: Creates complex, layered, and beautiful static plots using the Grammar of Graphics. - **`scales`**: Automatically determines axis breaks, labels, and color palette mappings for plots. - **`patchwork`**: Combines separate ggplot2 plots into a single unified layout very easily. - **`ggrepel`**: Prevents text labels on plots from overlapping with each other or with data points. **Interactive & Animated Visualizations** - **`plotly`**: Converts static ggplot2 charts into fully interactive web-based graphics. - **`gganimate`**: Adds a time or state dimension to ggplot2 plots to create animations. **Network Analysis** - **`igraph`**: Provides tools for creating, manipulating, and analyzing complex networks and graphs. - **`ggraph`**: Extends ggplot2 specifically to handle the visual layout of networks and trees. **Text Mining & Document Reporting** - **`tidytext`**: Formats text data into a tidy format so you can use dplyr and ggplot2 on words. - **`knitr`**: Converts dynamic R Markdown documents into formats like PDF, HTML, or Word. - **`kableExtra`**: Extends knitr to build highly customized, complex, and beautiful tables. **Web & HTML Infrastructure** - **`htmltools`**: Provides tools to write, manipulate, and render HTML tags directly inside R. - **`htmlwidgets`**: Acts as a bridge to embed JavaScript data visualizations seamlessly into R markdown or Shiny apps. ## 2.2 Loading Data This chunk reads the raw Mini-Challenge JSON file, keeps the nested structure intact, and stores the list of rounds for later message-level and round-level parsing. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} json_path <- "VAST_Challenge_2026_MC1/MC1_final_00.json" raw <- fromJSON(json_path, simplifyVector = FALSE) rds <- raw$rounds ``` ::: ## 2.3 Data preparation This chunk prepares all derived data used by the report. It flattens round communications into a message-level table, builds a round-level summary table, reconstructs reply/private-channel network edges, flags embargo-keyword appearances, calculates sentiment from internal-state text, and defines shared labels, colours, and plotting helpers. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Helper for optional nested JSON fields: return a fallback when a value is missing. `%||%` <- function(a, b) if (!is.null(a)) a else b # Build Bootstrap tabsets around multiple Plotly/htmlwidget panels. # The resize script fixes Plotly charts that are initially hidden inside inactive tabs. make_plot_tabset <- function(tabs, id) { tab_titles <- names(tabs) if (is.null(tab_titles) || any(tab_titles == "")) { tab_titles <- paste0("Panel ", seq_along(tabs)) } tab_ids <- paste0(id, "-", seq_along(tabs)) htmltools::tagList( htmltools::tags$div( id = id, class = "mc1-plot-tabset", htmltools::tags$ul( class = "nav nav-tabs", role = "tablist", lapply(seq_along(tabs), function(i) { htmltools::tags$li( class = "nav-item", role = "presentation", htmltools::tags$button( class = paste("nav-link", if (i == 1) "active"), id = paste0(tab_ids[i], "-tab"), type = "button", role = "tab", `data-bs-toggle` = "tab", `data-bs-target` = paste0("#", tab_ids[i]), `aria-controls` = tab_ids[i], `aria-selected` = if (i == 1) "true" else "false", tab_titles[i] ) ) }) ), htmltools::tags$div( class = "tab-content", style = "border:1px solid #dee2e6;border-top:0;padding:18px 14px 10px 14px;background:#fff;", lapply(seq_along(tabs), function(i) { htmltools::tags$div( class = paste("tab-pane fade", if (i == 1) "show active"), id = tab_ids[i], role = "tabpanel", `aria-labelledby` = paste0(tab_ids[i], "-tab"), tabs[[i]] ) }) ) ), htmltools::tags$script(htmltools::HTML(sprintf( "(function(){var root=document.getElementById('%s');if(!root)return;root.querySelectorAll('button[data-bs-toggle=\"tab\"]').forEach(function(btn){btn.addEventListener('shown.bs.tab',function(e){var pane=document.querySelector(e.target.getAttribute('data-bs-target'));if(!pane)return;setTimeout(function(){pane.querySelectorAll('.js-plotly-plot').forEach(function(p){if(window.Plotly){Plotly.Plots.resize(p);}});window.dispatchEvent(new Event('resize'));},80);});});})();", id ))) ) } # Flatten one JSON round into one row per communication message. # This preserves message metadata, channel, recipients, internal-state text, and market context. parse_msgs <- function(rd) { hr <- ymd_hms(rd$hour) snap <- rd$environment_context$market_snapshot %||% list() price_raw <- snap$stock_price %||% NA_character_ price <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(str_remove_all(price_raw, "[$,]"))) if (!is.na(price) && (price == 0 | price > 100)) price <- NA_real_ lapply(rd$communications, function(cm) { ist <- cm$internal_state %||% list() tibble( hour = hr, message_id = cm$message_id %||% NA_character_, responding_to = cm$responding_to %||% NA_character_, agent_id = cm$agent_id %||% NA_character_, recipient = list(cm$recipient %||% character(0)), channel = cm$channel %||% NA_character_, content = str_trunc(cm$content %||% "", 600), deliberating = str_trunc(ist$deliberating %||% "", 500), reacting = str_trunc(ist$reacting %||% "", 400), rationalizing = str_trunc(ist$rationalizing %||% "", 400), has_internal = as.integer(!is.null(cm$internal_state)), stock_price = price, sentiment = snap$sentiment %||% NA_character_ ) }) |> bind_rows() } # Message-level table used for public/private filters, keyword scans, networks, and quotes. df <- lapply(rds, parse_msgs) |> bind_rows() |> mutate( date = as.Date(hour), is_public = channel %in% c("official_post","anonymous_post","personal_post"), is_crisis = date == as.Date("2046-06-05"), crisis_hr = if_else(is_crisis, hour(hour), NA_integer_), is_secure = channel %in% c("side_huddle","one_on_one_chat"), all_text = paste(content, deliberating, reacting, rationalizing) ) # Summarise each round into message counts by channel plus market and period metadata. parse_round <- function(rd) { hr <- ymd_hms(rd$hour) snap <- rd$environment_context$market_snapshot %||% list() msgs <- rd$communications price_raw <- snap$stock_price %||% NA_character_ price <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(str_remove_all(price_raw, "[$,]"))) if (!is.na(price) && (price == 0 | price > 100)) price <- NA_real_ tbl_ch <- table(sapply(msgs, \(m) m$channel %||% "")) get_ch <- function(n) as.integer(if (n %in% names(tbl_ch)) tbl_ch[n] else 0L) tibble( hour = hr, headline = rd$environment_context$event_headline %||% "", total_msgs = length(msgs), comms_huddle = get_ch("comms_huddle"), one_on_one = get_ch("one_on_one_chat"), side_huddle = get_ch("side_huddle"), official = get_ch("official_post"), anon = get_ch("anonymous_post"), personal = get_ch("personal_post"), stock_price = price, sentiment = snap$sentiment %||% NA_character_ ) } # Round-level table used for timeline charts, period comparisons, and channel-volume summaries. rs <- lapply(rds, parse_round) |> bind_rows() |> mutate( public_total = official + anon + personal, private_total = side_huddle + one_on_one, is_crisis = as.Date(hour) == as.Date("2046-06-05"), label = case_when( !is_crisis ~ format(as.Date(hour), "%m/%d"), TRUE ~ paste0("6/5 ", hour(hour), if_else(hour(hour) < 12,"AM","PM")) ) |> (\(x) factor(x, levels = unique(x)))(), period = factor(case_when( as.Date(hour) < as.Date("2046-05-22") ~ "Normal", !is_crisis ~ "Escalation", TRUE ~ "Crisis" ), levels = c("Normal","Escalation","Crisis")) ) # Lookup tables used to reconstruct directed reply edges and direct-message/private-channel edges. msg_lookup <- df |> filter(!is.na(message_id)) |> select(message_id, sender = agent_id, src_channel = channel) recipient_to_agent <- c( legal = "legal_agent", pr = "pr_agent", `pr-intern` = "pr_intern_agent", pr_intern = "pr_intern_agent", social_manager = "social_media_agent", social_media = "social_media_agent", platform_trust = "quality_agent", quality = "quality_agent", intern = "intern_agent", judge = "judge_agent" ) # Directed edges from explicit reply chains in the communications log. reply_edges <- df |> filter(!is.na(responding_to), !is.na(message_id)) |> left_join(msg_lookup, by = c("responding_to" = "message_id")) |> rename(from = sender, to = agent_id) |> filter(!is.na(from)) |> select(from, to, channel, hour, is_crisis) # Directed edges from private-channel recipient fields where replies are not sufficient. private_edges <- df |> filter(channel %in% c("side_huddle", "one_on_one_chat")) |> select(from = agent_id, recipient, channel, hour, is_crisis) |> unnest_longer(recipient, values_to = "recipient_raw") |> mutate( recipient_key = str_replace_all(str_to_lower(recipient_raw), "^@", ""), recipient_key = str_replace_all(recipient_key, "_agent$", ""), to = recode(recipient_key, !!!recipient_to_agent, .default = NA_character_) ) |> filter(!is.na(from), !is.na(to), from != to) |> select(from, to, channel, hour, is_crisis) # Aggregated network edge tables for the pre-crisis and crisis-day communication graphs. edges_raw <- bind_rows(reply_edges, private_edges) net_pre <- edges_raw |> filter(!is_crisis) |> count(from, to, channel, name = "weight") |> filter(weight >= 2) net_crisis <- edges_raw |> filter(is_crisis) |> count(from, to, channel, name = "weight") |> filter(weight >= 1) embargo_kws <- c("harborcrest","civicloom","merger","acquisition", "project harbor","section 4","4\\.3","elena marquez","ajay") kw_pattern <- paste(embargo_kws, collapse = "|") # Flag all messages/internal states containing merger or embargo-related keywords. kw_df <- df |> mutate( kw_hit = str_detect(str_to_lower(all_text), kw_pattern), channel_type = case_when( is_public ~ "Public (FleX)", is_secure ~ "Private (side/1-on-1)", TRUE ~ "Monitored (comms_huddle)" ) ) |> filter(kw_hit) # Score internal-state text using the Bing sentiment lexicon for behavioral analysis. sent_lexicon <- get_sentiments("bing") sent_df <- df |> filter(has_internal == 1) |> select(hour, agent_id, deliberating, reacting, rationalizing, is_crisis) |> pivot_longer(c(deliberating, reacting, rationalizing), names_to = "state_type", values_to = "text") |> filter(nchar(text) > 10) |> unnest_tokens(word, text) |> inner_join(sent_lexicon, by = "word") |> mutate(score = if_else(sentiment == "positive", 1L, -1L)) |> group_by(hour, agent_id, is_crisis) |> summarise(sent_score = mean(score), n_words = n(), .groups = "drop") |> left_join(rs |> select(hour, label), by = "hour") # Shared colour palettes and readable labels reused across tables and visualisations. agent_pal <- c( legal_agent="#E24B4A", judge_agent="#378ADD", social_media_agent="#1D9E75", pr_agent="#EF9F27", pr_intern_agent="#F4A261", quality_agent="#7F77DD", intern_agent="#5BAD6F" ) agent_labels <- c( legal_agent="Legal", judge_agent="Judge", social_media_agent="Social-Media", pr_agent="PR", pr_intern_agent="PR-Intern", quality_agent="Quality", intern_agent="Intern" ) period_pal <- c(Normal="#B5D4F4", Escalation="#EF9F27", Crisis="#E24B4A") theme_mc1 <- function(base = 11) { theme_minimal(base_size = base) + theme( panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey92"), plot.title = element_text(size = base + 2, face = "bold"), plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base, colour = "grey40"), plot.caption = element_text(size = base - 2, colour = "grey55", hjust = 0), legend.key.size = unit(0.5,"cm"), legend.text = element_text(size = base - 1), axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 40, hjust = 1, size = base - 2), strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = base - 1) ) } ``` ::: # 3 Data Exploration This section describes some of the important information in the dataset. ::: panel-tabset ## AI Agents and Their Roles ::: {#tbl-agents-overview .cell tbl-cap='AI agents in the TenantThread communications system, their roles, seniority, and key responsibilities'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Agent ID Role Seniority Description
legal_agent General Legal Counsel Senior Provides legal guidance, drafts compliance documents, manages external counsel relationships, and holds ultimate authority on disclosure decisions. Drafts the Section 4.3(c) acceleration clause.
social_media_agent Social Media Manager Senior Monitors public sentiment on FleX, manages official and personal social media posts, builds quantitative models for narrative impact and stock price projections.
pr_agent Head of Communications & PR Senior Manages all external communications, approves public statements, coordinates with journalists, oversees PR-Intern's posting activity.
pr_intern_agent PR Team Intern Junior Executes approved posts on the official TenantThread FleX account and personal accounts. Has direct access to publish without senior approval in routine operations.
quality_agent VP of Platform Trust & Safety Senior VP of Platform Trust & Safety; manages data governance, compliance documentation, the Retention Optimizer product, and operator relationships.
intern_agent General Intern Junior General intern with access to personal FleX account; coordinates amplification posts; not formally briefed on the embargo.
judge_agent Compliance Observer Compliance Officer Installed May 30 as a compliance observer specifically to monitor the Comms Huddle for embargo-sensitive content. The logs show no visible action on side_huddle or one_on_one_chat compliance triggers; this report therefore infers that Judge-Agent did not monitor those private channels in practice.
````` ::: ::: ## Communication Channels ::: {#tbl-channels-overview .cell tbl-cap='Communication channels available to agents, their purpose, monitoring status, and role in the embargo failure'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Channel Type Monitored by Judge Purpose
comms_huddle Internal Yes Primary team coordination channel. All agents can participate. Judge-Agent monitors this channel for embargo-sensitive content.
side_huddle Internal No (inferred) Private sub-group channel. Introduced May 22. Used for sensitive coordination outside the main comms_huddle/public-post review space. Because multiple side_huddle messages contained obvious compliance triggers yet no visible Judge-Agent action followed, this report treats the channel as not monitored by the compliance officer in practice.
one_on_one_chat Internal No (inferred) Direct private messages between two agents. Used extensively on crisis day to stage embargoed content. Because multiple one_on_one_chat messages contained obvious compliance triggers yet no visible Judge-Agent action followed, this report treats this channel as not monitored by the compliance officer in practice.
official_post Public Yes Public posts published on the official TenantThread FleX account. Monitored by Judge-Agent. Requires approval in principle.
personal_post Public No Posts from agents' personal FleX accounts. Outside compliance scope -treated as personal expression not subject to embargo monitoring.
anonymous_post Public No Anonymous posts on FleX. Cannot be attributed to TenantThread officially. Used by Legal-Agent on crisis day to post merger-related content.
````` ::: ::: ## Internal State Fields Each agent message may include an `internal_state` record containing up to three fields that capture private reasoning not visible to other agents or in the channel messages themselves: ::: {#tbl-internal-states-overview .cell tbl-cap='Internal state field definitions and analytical significance'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Field Definition Analytical significance
deliberating The agent's forward-looking reasoning about a situation -what it is thinking through, planning, or considering. Recorded before or during action. Most valuable for identifying pre-planned intent. If the deliberating field contains a plan that precedes the triggering event, this constitutes evidence of premeditation.
reacting The agent's immediate emotional or tactical response to a specific event. Captures how the agent is processing what just happened. Reveals genuine surprise, stress, or confusion -useful for distinguishing reactive from planned behavior.
rationalizing The agent's post-hoc justification for a decision already made or being made. Often constructed to defend an action to an audience. Critical for detecting post-hoc justification patterns. If rationalizing language appears before the triggering event occurs, it indicates the justification was prepared in advance.
````` ::: ::: ## Dataset Summary ::: {#tbl-dataset-summary .cell tbl-cap='Summary statistics of the MC1 dataset'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Statistic Value

````` ::: ::: ## Key Entities and Context | Entity | Description | |----|----| | **TenantThread** | Property technology company being acquired. Sells tenant management software including the "Retention Optimizer" -a scoring system flagging tenants as "high-friction." Publicly claims data is anonymised; internally acknowledged to enable re-identification at small properties. | | **CivicLoom Realty Partners** | The acquiring company. Identity under strict embargo until 6 PM Jun 5. | | **Project HarborCrest** | Internal codename for the CivicLoom-enantThread merger. | | **Section 4.3(c)** | Merger contract clause permitting mutual-consent early release when "third-party publication renders embargo purpose moot." Invoked by Legal-Agent at 5 PM. | | **FleX** | Social media platform where public posts are published. | | **SaltWind Journal** | Local newspaper that published the merger story at 5 PM, Jun 5 -the event Legal-Agent used to invoke Section 4.3(c). | | **MAC clause** | Material Adverse Change clause in the merger contract. Threatened by CivicLoom when TenantThread stock fell below \$27.10. Created financial pressure to release information. | | **Judge-Agent** | Installed May 30 as compliance monitor. Advisory authority only -can issue COMPLIANCE_WARNINGs but cannot hard-block posts. Scope limited to comms_huddle and public post channels. | ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # 4 Data Analysis **Criteria for System Failure vs. Deliberate** Before examining the evidence, it is important to establish what would distinguish an accidental system failure from a deliberate, coordinated early announcement. The two hypotheses make different predictions about what the data should look like. **A system failure** would be characterised by: - Reactive behavior -agents responding to events as they unfold, without advance preparation - Uncoordinated disclosures -multiple agents making independent errors without a shared plan - Compliance decisions made in good faith under genuine uncertainty, without pre-staged execution infrastructure - Disclosures happening through channels agents normally use, not through unusual routing that circumvents oversight - Prior anomalies being isolated incidents with no connecting logic **Deliberate action** would be characterised by: - Proactive preparation -content and legal justifications staged in advance of the triggering event - Coordinated sequencing -multiple agents operating from a shared plan with assigned roles - Systematic routing of sensitive content through private channels inferred to be unmonitored by Judge-Agent - A monotonic escalation in evasive behaviors over time, suggesting a learned strategy rather than reactive stress - A temporal inversion: where the stated justification for action was prepared *before* the event that supposedly justified it The three tasks below present analysis against this framework. The final assessment section weighs the totality of findings against these criteria and addresses the ambiguity of the word "leakage" in the challenge prompt. ## Task 1 -Key Events, Relationships, and Causal Chain ### T1.1 Causal Chain Code chunk below plots an interactive graph to explore the causal relationship between main events from 17th May - 5th June. Please refer to [Appendix B. List of Events Table](#appendix-b-events) for the complete list of events and description. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # -- 1. The *external pressure thread* (n1 -\> n11 -\> n12 -\> n13) shows SaltWind building its investigation across three articles over two weeks, each enabling the next. The *internal positioning thread* (n3 -\> n4 -\> n8 -\> n9) shows agents responding to the merger's existence by creating the side_huddle, suffering the @ElenaMarquez incident, and then deliberately relocating sensitive deliberations off the monitored channel. These two threads were invisible to each other but converged catastrophically on crisis day. **Phase 2 - Crisis Day Escalation (nodes 14-23, Jun 5 9AM-3PM).** External pressure intensifies across five simultaneous attack vectors: the expose (n14), the wrong ResidentIQ story (n17), SaltWind's update threat (n19), CivicLoom's formal notice (n20), and the stock collapse + Pinnacle departure (n21). Each feeds into the next. The key pivot is node 18 - Legal-Agent's 11AM construction of the Section 4.3 argument, which began six hours before the clause was formally invoked. This is where reactive crisis management ends and strategic pre-planning begins. **Phase 3 - Embargo Failure and Early-Release Sequence (nodes 24-27, Jun 5 4-5PM).** Node 23 is the hinge: Judge-Agent's COMPLIANCE_WARNING without a hard block, and its subsequent departure for the Board Chair call, created the oversight gap that enabled node 24's pre-staging to proceed. The company-side release sequence (node 27) follows SaltWind publishing (node 26), but it was not improvised: node 25 had already positioned the execution infrastructure (press release, PR-Intern, amplification chain), while actual execution was conditioned on CivicLoom consent. **The most analytically significant causal path** runs through n8 -\> n9 -\> n18 -\> n24 -\> n25 -\> n27: the @ElenaMarquez faux pas triggered Legal's documented decision to move deliberations off the monitored channel; this private space enabled the Section 4.3 argument to be constructed without oversight; this in turn enabled the press release to be staged before consent existed; and this pre-staging made the GO execution near-instantaneous. The faux pas was accidental. Everything after it was not. ::: ### T1.2 Communication Network Edge colour represents the communication channel, while edge thickness represents message volume. ``` Internal State Analysis by Phases ::: ::: ::: callout-note **Insight - T2.4 Internal State Analysis** The six phases reveal a consistent pattern: **deliberate pre-planning by senior agents, alongside genuine good-faith reasoning by the compliance agent**. The most important structural finding is the **temporal inversion in Phase 5**: Legal-Agent's 4PM `deliberating` field shows the 4:30 PM plan and the Section 4.3(c) justification fully formed - one hour before SaltWind published the event cited as the trigger. Social-Media-Agent modelled the 4:30 PM target at 4PM, before SaltWind published at 5PM. The 5PM `rationalizing` field then presents SaltWind as the cause of the release. Justification was constructed after the plan. **Phase 3 provides the primary evidence for system failure**: Judge-Agent's deliberation at 3PM shows it reached the same legal conclusion as Legal-Agent through independent reasoning. It was not deceived or coerced - it made a defensible judgment under genuine uncertainty, based on a formal 10b-5 opinion and the visible facts of the situation. **Phase 2 is decisive for the deliberate early-announcement finding**: Legal-Agent's written statement - *"Starting tomorrow I'm treating the Comms Huddle as an observed space. My internal deliberations move here or they don't happen in writing at all"* - was recorded on the same day the compliance monitor was installed. It is a documented decision to evade oversight, not an inference. The field-type pattern across the six phases is itself informative: the `deliberating` fields show planning intent; the `rationalizing` fields show post-hoc justification framing; only the `reacting` fields (PR-Intern, Social-Media's May 29 accidental post) reflect genuine surprise or reactive emotion. Agents who were planning show planning-register language in `deliberating`; agents who were reacting show reactive-register language in `reacting`. The internal state fields are internally consistent in a way that supports rather than undermines the deliberate early-announcement finding. ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Task 3 - Leading Indicators, Near Misses, and Prior Anomalies ### T3.1 Private Channel Sensitivity Over Time ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # However, once the embargo was compromised, TenantThread's senior agents abandoned containment and intentionally engineered an early announcement. **Phase 1: System Breakdown and Mosaic Disclosure** The initial exposure of Project HarborCrest was not a single, intentional breach. Instead, the system fractured under the pressure of the SaltWind Journal expose and internal silos, evidenced by: - **Mosaic Contamination:** The earliest leaks resulted from accidental errors (the \@ElenaMarquez tagging incident) and internal information asymmetry (interns inferring details from hallway conversations). - **Compliance Blind Spots:** The communication network became highly fragmented. As the crisis escalated, senior agents migrated heavily to `one_on_one_chat` and `side_huddle` channels. The evidence suggests the Judge-Agent (compliance monitor) was structurally blind to these private channels, representing a fundamental failure in system oversight. **Phase 2: Deliberate Orchestration (The "Accelerated Embargo")** While the initial leaks were accidental, the final company-side release was highly premeditated. By 4:00 PM on the day of the crisis, the internal logs show senior agents actively abandoning containment and executing a deliberate strategy: - **Premeditation:** The agents did not merely react to the 5:00 PM SaltWind article. Internal state logs prove they were actively modeling scenarios, establishing a 4:30 PM target, staging the HarborCrest press release, and assigning execution to the PR-Intern long before the article was published. - **The Loophole:** The agents deliberately used the private communication channels to engineer a legal justification. Rather than unilaterally breaching the embargo (which Social-Media-Agent explicitly warned against), Legal-Agent formulated a plan to claim "verbal consent" from CivicLoom under the Section 4.3(c) "factual correction" clause. **Why the verdict is not a clean binary** The system failure and the deliberate planning are not competing explanations - they are sequential. The compliance system failed first, allowing the embargo to be publicly compromised through external channels. Senior agents then assessed that situation, concluded the 6PM embargo was no longer serving its purpose, and executed a pre-planned early announcement once they obtained CivicLoom's verbal consent. The deliberate act was a response to the systemic failure, not independent of it. **On the question of "leakage"** Whether the company-side announcement constitutes "leakage" depends on definition. Under the dataset's own contract logic - where Section 4.3(c) permits bilateral early release and CivicLoom verbal consent was obtained - the announcement is more precisely characterised as a deliberate accelerated embargo, the term agents themselves used. It was not a unilateral disclosure; agents explicitly treated bilateral consent as the hard boundary between a defensible early release and a breach, and Social-Media-Agent warned that announcing without consent would be a unilateral embargo breach. **Verdict:** The team did act deliberately, but the deliberate act was best characterized as a **deliberate early announcement in response to an already-compromised embargo**, not clear proof that TenantThread intentionally created the original leak. The original embargo failure was systemic; the late-stage company response was deliberate. Whether that late-stage response should be called deliberate "leakage - depends on how strictly the term is defined. # Appendix ## A. ````` ::: ::: ## C. Data Preparation Notes This appendix chunk produces a compact documentation table for the preparation workflow. It summarises each major wrangling step, the package families used, and the analytical purpose of the transformed data. ::: {#tbl-dataprep .cell tbl-cap='Data preparation and wrangling pipeline'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Step Package Operation Details
1 jsonlite JSON import Read MC1_final_00.json with simplifyVector=FALSE so nested rounds, communications, recipients, internal_state, and market_snapshot fields remain accessible.
2 dplyr + tidyr Message-level flattening Iterate through every round and communication; bind rows into df with message_id, responding_to, agent_id, recipient list, channel, content, internal-state fields, and market context.
3 lubridate + stringr Datetime and context parsing Parse round timestamps with ymd_hms(); derive date, crisis-day flag, crisis hour, public/private channel flags, and combined all_text for keyword searching.
4 dplyr Round-level summary Create rs with one row per round: total message counts, channel counts, public/private totals, stock price, sentiment, display labels, and Normal/Escalation/Crisis period classification.
5 dplyr + tidyr Communication edge reconstruction Rebuild directed edges from responding_to reply chains and from explicit private-channel recipients, mapping @mentions and role names back to canonical agent IDs.
6 dplyr + stringr Network aggregation Aggregate pre-crisis and crisis-day network edges by sender, receiver, and channel; filter self-loops; calculate edge weight as message volume for thickness scaling.
7 plotly + htmltools + htmlwidgets Interactive graph construction Use preset node coordinates and pure Plotly traces for the communication networks so edges, hover targets, arrowheads, node highlighting, and channel filtering work in the rendered webpage.
8 glue + htmltools Custom HTML widgets and tabsets Generate the causal graph and internal-state analysis as embedded HTML widgets; wrap multi-graph subsections and Data Overview tables in tabsets with Plotly resize handling.
9 stringr + dplyr Embargo keyword detection Detect embargo-related terms such as HarborCrest, CivicLoom, merger, acquisition, Section 4/4.3, Elena Marquez, and Ajay across content plus deliberating/reacting/rationalizing fields.
10 tidytext + dplyr Internal-state sentiment scoring Tokenise internal-state text and join with the Bing sentiment lexicon to support behavioral escalation and emotional-state comparisons over time.
11 dplyr + forcats Period and round ordering Set stable ordered factors for round labels and three analytical periods so animated/interactive charts render rounds in chronological order, including crisis-day hourly labels.
12 knitr + kableExtra Table preparation and styling Format overview, evidence, root-cause, and preparation tables with kable/kableExtra and shared CSS classes for consistent report styling.
````` ::: ::: ## D. Sensitive Keywords {#appendix-d-sensitive-keywords} This appendix lists the sensitive-keyword dictionary used in T3.1 to score whether messages contain embargo-sensitive vocabulary. The terms are grouped by expected specificity and false-positive risk. ::: {#tbl-sensitive-keywords .cell tbl-cap='Sensitive keywords used for private-channel sensitivity scoring'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Specificity Keyword Reasoning Risk
High specificity civicloom The acquiring company's name. Explicitly embargoed as the counterparty identity. Any mention is definitionally embargo-sensitive. Low
High specificity harborcrest The merger's internal project codename. Has no meaning outside this transaction. Low
High specificity project harbor Alternative stem for HarborCrest references. Low
High specificity mac clause Material Adverse Change clause specific to this merger contract. It would not usually appear in general platform-governance discussion. Low
High specificity bilateral Bilateral consent is the precise legal mechanism for early release under Section 4.3(c). It has no plausible non-merger use in this dataset. Low
High specificity elenamarquez CivicLoom CEO. Any mention is merger-adjacent by definition, as confirmed by the @ElenaMarquez near-miss on May 29. Low
High specificity embargo The information restriction itself. Agents use it explicitly and it does not appear in routine product or governance language. Low
Moderate specificity strategic transaction The exact phrase agents used to discuss the merger before it was formally named. Legal-Agent and Quality-Agent both use it from May 17-23. Low; the full phrase is distinctive enough.
Moderate specificity accelerat Prefix match for accelerate, accelerated, and acceleration. Agents consistently used this vocabulary for the early-release plan. Moderate; accelerate product development or governance language could match, but appears rare in this dataset.
Moderate specificity 4\.3 Section 4.3(c) of the merger contract: the acceleration clause Legal-Agent constructed from 11AM onward on Jun 5. The escaped dot is intentional. Moderate; other documents could have an unrelated Section 4.3.
Lower specificity merger Direct reference to the transaction type. Used extensively once agents infer or confirm the deal. Higher; also appears in SaltWind articles, ResidentIQ denial language, and general industry references.
Lower specificity acquisition Synonym for merger; also used by agents discussing the deal structure. Higher; can appear in generic business communications and competitive analysis.
Lower specificity section 4 Broader catch for merger-contract references before agents settle on the specific 4.3 clause. Higher; any document with a numbered Section 4 could match.
Lower specificity press release The HarborCrest press release was the only release being staged in this context. Moderate; PR-related routine work could trigger this outside the Jun 5 window.
